title: Delhi Metro Phase V(B): 7 New Corridors, 65 New Stations, and What It Means for You (2026)
description: Delhi CM Rekha Gupta announced 7 new metro corridors under Phase V(B) covering 97 km and 65 stations at Rs 48,204 crore. See every route, station list, interchange points, and timeline in simple language.
body:
Big news for Delhi Metro commuters. On May 3, 2026, Delhi Chief Minister Rekha Gupta announced seven brand-new corridors under **Delhi Metro Phase V(B)** at the Delhi Metro Rail Corporation's (DMRC) 32nd Foundation Day. These are not small extensions. Together, they add **97.158 km of new metro track** and **65 new stations** across Delhi, at a total estimated cost of **Rs 48,204.56 crore**.

If you live in west Delhi, north Delhi, south Delhi, or east Delhi, there is a very good chance one of these seven corridors is coming to your neighbourhood. This guide breaks down every single corridor in simple language, tells you which areas benefit the most, when it will be ready, and what it means for your daily commute.

## **Why This Announcement Matters**

Delhi's metro network has grown from a small 8.4 km line in 2002 to one of the largest metro systems in the world today. Currently, the Delhi Metro runs approximately **395 km of track** with **289 stations** across 12 lines.

But despite this size, large parts of Delhi still do not have metro access. Outer areas, rapidly developing localities, and some densely packed parts of central Delhi still rely entirely on buses, autos, and personal vehicles.

Phase V(B) is designed to fix this. It targets the gaps, the parts of the city that have been waiting for metro connectivity for years. CM Rekha Gupta described the Metro as the _lifeline of Delhi_ and said that every rupee invested in metro projects is being used transparently for long-term public benefit.

She also noted that the Delhi Metro has earned carbon credits from the United Nations and has helped reduce over **6 lakh (600,000) tonnes of carbon emissions**, making it not just a transport solution, but an environmental one too.

## **The Big Numbers at a Glance**

Before we go corridor by corridor, here is the full picture of what Phase V(B) brings:

| **Detail** | **Number** |
| --- | --- |
| Total new corridors | 7 |
| Total new track length | 97.158 km |
| Total new stations | 65 |
| Estimated total cost | Rs 48,204.56 crore |
| Priority corridors | 4 (target completion: 2029) |
| Remaining corridors | 3 (to be developed in phases after 2029) |

Four of the seven corridors have been marked as **priority projects**. CM Rekha Gupta has set a target of completing these four by 2029. The remaining three will follow after that in phases.

## **All 7 New Corridors Explained, One by One**

### **Corridor 1: Dhansa Bus Stand (Najafgarh) to Nangloi**

- **Length:** 11.859 km
- **Type:** Fully elevated, independent corridor
- **Number of stations:** 9

This corridor connects Najafgarh, one of Delhi's far western areas, with Nangloi. Najafgarh is a large, growing locality that has been without metro access for a long time. This corridor will finally bring fast rail connectivity to residents of Najafgarh and the villages and localities in between.

Being a fully elevated corridor, it will be faster and cheaper to construct than underground sections. This makes it a strong candidate for priority status.

**Who benefits:** Residents of Najafgarh, Dhansa, and areas along the Nangloi route who currently have to rely entirely on road transport to reach the rest of Delhi.

### **Corridor 2: Central Secretariat to Kishangarh**

- **Length:** 15.969 km
- **Type:** Mostly underground
- **Number of stations:** 10

This is one of the more significant corridors in the list. It runs from Central Secretariat, a major interchange station in central Delhi already connected to the Yellow, Violet, and Magenta lines, all the way to Kishangarh, which is on the Golden Line.

The corridor serves **RK Puram, New Moti Bagh, and JNU** (Jawaharlal Nehru University), areas that are densely populated with government employees, students, and professionals, but currently have limited metro access.

**Interchange points:**

- Central Secretariat (Violet, Yellow, and Magenta lines)
- Moti Bagh (Pink Line)
- RK Puram (Magenta Line)
- Kishangarh (Golden Line)

The multi-line interchange at Central Secretariat makes this corridor an important connector. If you currently use the Yellow Line to get to Rajiv Chowk and then change to reach South Delhi, this corridor could offer a more direct route. Speaking of interchanges, if you're not familiar with how interchange stations work at busy hubs, our guide on[ navigating the Rajiv Chowk Yellow and Blue Line interchange](https://metroguideindia.com/blog/rajiv-chowk-yellow-blue-line-interchange-guide.html) is a useful read before you plan any complex metro route.

**Who benefits:** Government employees in the South Block and Secretariat area, JNU students, and residents of RK Puram and Moti Bagh.

### **Corridor 3: Samaypur Badli to Narela DDA Sports Complex**

- **Length:** 12.89 km
- **Type:** Fully elevated extension of existing Line 2 (Yellow Line)
- **Number of stations:** 8

This corridor is an extension of the existing Yellow Line northward from Samaypur Badli, which is currently the northernmost station on the Yellow Line, up to Narela DDA Sports Complex.

It will pass through **Siraspur, Khera Kalan, Holambi Kalan, Alipur, and Shahpur Garhi** before reaching DSIIDC Narela.

Narela is a large industrial and residential township developed by DDA in the far north of Delhi. It has been largely disconnected from the metro for years. This extension brings the Yellow Line all the way to Narela, making it dramatically easier for workers, students, and families living there to travel to the rest of the city.

**Interchange points:**

- Siraspur (Yellow Line, this is where the extension joins the existing network)
- Narela DDA Sports Complex (Red Line)

**Who benefits:** Residents and workers of Narela, Holambi Kalan, Alipur, and surrounding areas. This is a very significant addition for North Delhi's outer zones.

### **Corridor 4: Kirti Nagar to Palam**

- **Length:** 9.967 km
- **Type:** Mixed, 8.397 km underground + 1.57 km elevated
- **Number of stations:** 6 (5 underground, 1 elevated)

This corridor extends Line 5 from Kirti Nagar southward to Palam. It serves West Delhi's residential and industrial areas, passing through **Saraswati Garden, Mayapuri Industrial Area Phase III, IITM Janakpuri, and Sagarpur**.

The mostly underground alignment means it will tunnel through dense urban areas where elevated construction is not possible.

**Interchange points:**

- Mayapuri (Pink Line)
- Palam (Magenta Line)

Connecting Kirti Nagar to the Magenta Line at Palam creates a new cross-city link for west Delhi commuters and reduces the need to travel all the way into central Delhi to change lines.

**Who benefits:** Workers at Mayapuri Industrial Area, residents of Saraswati Garden, Janakpuri, and Sagarpur, and anyone who needs a more direct west Delhi metro route.

### **Corridor 5: Jor Bagh to Mithapur**

- **Length:** 16.991 km
- **Type:** Mixed underground and elevated
- **Number of stations:** 12 (7 underground, 5 elevated)

This is the longest of the seven corridors and one of the most strategically important. It runs from Jor Bagh in south-central Delhi all the way to Mithapur in the far south, passing through **Lodhi Colony, Old Ishwar Nagar, Ali Village, Jaitpur, Moladband, and Mithapur**.

This corridor creates a new north-south spine through south and south-east Delhi, areas that are densely populated but poorly connected to the current metro grid.

**Interchange points:**

- Jor Bagh (Yellow Line)
- Lajpat Nagar (Violet Line and Pink Line)
- NSIC Okhla (Magenta Line)
- Jasola Apollo (Violet Line)
- Madanpur Khadar (Golden Line extension)

The number of interchanges on this corridor is the highest of any of the seven. This makes it a very powerful connector across multiple metro lines.

**Who benefits:** Residents of Lodhi Colony, Lajpat Nagar, Okhla, Jasola, Jaitpur, and Mithapur. Also very useful for people who need to travel between South Delhi destinations without going through central Delhi.

### **Corridor 6: Shastri Park to Mayur Vihar Phase III**

- **Length:** 13.197 km
- **Type:** Independent corridor, 8.99 km underground + 4.207 km elevated
- **Number of stations:** 8

This corridor covers east Delhi's underserved zones. Running from Shastri Park to Mayur Vihar Phase III, it passes through **Kailash Nagar, Gandhi Nagar market, Geeta Colony, Patparganj (Mother Dairy), and New Kondli**.

East Delhi is one of the most densely populated parts of the city. Many of its inner localities, like Gandhi Nagar and Geeta Colony, are large residential and commercial areas that are not directly on any existing metro line.

**Interchange points:**

- Shastri Park (Red Line)
- Nirman Vihar (Blue Line)
- Trilokpuri (Pink Line)

**Who benefits:** Residents of Geeta Colony, Gandhi Nagar, Kailash Nagar, Patparganj, and Mayur Vihar Phase III. Also highly useful for traders and shoppers in the Gandhi Nagar market area, one of Delhi's largest wholesale textile markets.

### **Corridor 7: Keshavpuram to Rohini Sector 34**

- **Length:** 16.285 km
- **Type:** Fully elevated, independent corridor
- **Number of stations:** 12

This corridor runs through north-west Delhi, connecting Keshavpuram with Rohini Sector 34. It passes through **West Shalimar Bagh, East Pitampura, Rohini Sector 16, Delhi Technological University (DTU), and Rohini Sector 29**.

DTU is one of Delhi's most prominent engineering universities. Students and staff currently have to rely on limited bus routes or combine multiple metro journeys to reach it. This corridor gives DTU a direct metro connection.

**Interchange points:**

- Keshavpuram (Red Line)
- North Pitampura (Pink Line)
- Rohini Sector 34 (Red Line)

Being fully elevated, this corridor will also be among the faster ones to construct once approved.

**Who benefits:** Students and staff of Delhi Technological University, residents of Pitampura, Rohini, Shalimar Bagh, and workers commuting along the north-west corridor.

## **Complete Summary Table: All 7 Corridors at a Glance**

| **#** | **Route** | **Length** | **Stations** | **Type** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Dhansa Bus Stand → Nangloi | 11.859 km | 9 | Fully elevated |
| 2 | Central Secretariat → Kishangarh | 15.969 km | 10 | Mostly underground |
| 3 | Samaypur Badli → Narela DDA Sports Complex | 12.89 km | 8 | Fully elevated |
| 4 | Kirti Nagar → Palam | 9.967 km | 6 | Mixed |
| 5 | Jor Bagh → Mithapur | 16.991 km | 12 | Mixed |
| 6 | Shastri Park → Mayur Vihar Phase III | 13.197 km | 8 | Mixed |
| 7 | Keshavpuram → Rohini Sector 34 | 16.285 km | 12 | Fully elevated |
| **Total** |  | **97.158 km** | **65** |  |

## **Which Corridors Are Priority Projects?**

CM Rekha Gupta confirmed that **four of the seven corridors** have been identified as priority projects with a target completion date of **2029**. The remaining three will be developed in phases after the priority corridors are completed.

She also directed the DMRC to prepare and submit **revised Detailed Project Reports (DPRs)** separately for the priority and non-priority corridors. This is the formal process that comes before construction begins.

The corridors most likely to be on the priority list are those that serve the largest underserved populations, have simpler construction profiles (like fully elevated routes), or connect to major existing interchange stations. However, the official priority list will be confirmed when the DMRC submits its revised DPRs.

## **What Happens Before Construction Starts?**

It is important to understand that an announcement of corridors is not the same as construction starting. Here is how the process works:

1. **DPR preparation:** The DMRC prepares a Detailed Project Report for each corridor, including alignment maps, station designs, cost estimates, and environmental impact assessments.
2. **State approval:** The Delhi government reviews and approves the DPRs.
3. **Central government approval:** The Union Cabinet approves the project and funding structure.
4. **Tendering:** Contracts are put out to tender and awarded to construction companies.
5. **Land acquisition and civil work:** Land is acquired where needed, and construction begins.
6. **Testing and commissioning:** Completed sections are tested and approved by safety authorities before opening.

For Phase V(B), the DPR preparation process is now being ordered. Construction on priority corridors is targeted to begin as soon as approvals are in place, with a 2029 completion target for the fast-track routes.

## **Which Parts of Delhi Benefit the Most?**

Looking at all seven corridors together, here are the parts of Delhi that gain the most from Phase V(B):

- **Outer West Delhi (Najafgarh area):** Corridor 1 finally gives Najafgarh metro access. This has been one of the most requested metro extensions for years.
- **South Delhi (JNU, RK Puram, Okhla, Jasola, Mithapur):** Corridors 2 and 5 together bring strong south Delhi coverage, especially for areas near JNU and the south-east.
- **Far North Delhi (Narela):** Corridor 3 is transformative for Narela, which is a large township that has been completely disconnected from the metro network.
- **West Delhi (Mayapuri, Janakpuri, Palam):** Corridor 4 adds a critical west-side link between the Blue/Green Line area and the Magenta Line.
- **East Delhi (Gandhi Nagar, Geeta Colony, Mayur Vihar Phase III):** Corridor 6 fills the east Delhi gap that has existed since the metro's early years.
- **North-West Delhi (Rohini, Pitampura, DTU):** Corridor 7 brings metro access to DTU and expands coverage in Rohini's outer sectors.

## **How to Prepare for the New Corridors: Tips for Future Commuters**

The corridors are still in the planning and approval stage. But it is a good time to start understanding how the metro system works, especially if you are going to be a new metro commuter once these corridors open.

Here are two things that will help you a lot:

- **Learn about ticketing options.** The Delhi Metro offers Smart Cards and QR code tickets. Each has its own advantages. Smart Cards are faster for daily commuters, while QR tickets are convenient for occasional travellers. Our detailed comparison of [Delhi Metro Smart Card vs QR Ticket](https://metroguideindia.com/blog/delhi-metro-smart-card-vs-qr-ticket.html) explains everything you need to know before your first ride, including how to buy, recharge, and which option saves more money over time.
- **Understand how interchange stations work.** Several of the new Phase V(B) corridors connect at major interchange stations like Central Secretariat, Lajpat Nagar, and Shastri Park. Knowing how to navigate these busy hubs saves time. Our guide on [using the Rajiv Chowk Yellow and Blue Line interchange](https://metroguideindia.com/blog/rajiv-chowk-yellow-blue-line-interchange-guide.html) is a practical walkthrough of one of Delhi's most-used interchange points, and the skills you learn there apply to any major interchange station.

## **What Else Was Announced on Foundation Day?**

The Phase V(B) expansion was not the only announcement at DMRC's 32nd Foundation Day. A few other important points came out of the event:

- **Cloth Bank Initiative:** CM Rekha Gupta proposed setting up cloth banks at metro stations to facilitate the recycling of used garments. This is a sustainability initiative that uses metro stations as community service points.
- **Carbon Credits Recognition:** The CM highlighted that the Delhi Metro has received carbon credits from the United Nations and reduced over 6 lakh tonnes of carbon emissions. This recognition places Delhi Metro among the world's greener transit systems.
- **Last-Mile Connectivity Focus:** The government announced a focus on improving last-mile connectivity alongside route expansion. This includes better feeder buses, e-rickshaw services, and integration with other transport modes at metro stations.
- **Financial Discipline:** The CM emphasised that all metro investments are being made transparently, with a long-term vision and no wastage of funds.
- **Japan's Role:** Senior representatives from the Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA) and the Embassy of Japan were present at the event, underscoring Japan's continued role in funding and technically supporting Delhi Metro's growth.

## **Delhi Metro's Growth: A Quick History to Understand Where We Are**

To understand just how significant Phase V(B) is, it helps to know how far the Delhi Metro has come:

- **2002:** Delhi Metro launched its first line, a short stretch in west Delhi.
- **2010:** Major expansion for the Commonwealth Games brought the network to over 190 km.
- **2019–2023:** Phase IV construction started, adding more corridors across south and west Delhi.
- **2025:** Phase V(A) approved by the Union Cabinet in December 2025, adding 13 stations across three corridors, bringing the total to approximately 400 km.
- **May 2026:** Phase V(B) corridors announced, 97 km and 65 new stations to follow.

Each phase has brought Delhi closer to a metro network that covers the whole city. Phase V(B), once completed, will make significant progress toward that goal.

## **What This Means for Daily Life in Delhi**

When Phase V(B) corridors eventually open, the impact on daily life in Delhi will be significant:

- **Less traffic:** Every person who switches from a car or scooter to the metro reduces traffic on Delhi's roads. With 65 new stations reaching previously unconnected areas, a large number of new commuters will shift to the metro.
- **More affordable commuting:** Metro travel is significantly cheaper than petrol, CNG, or cab costs for daily travel. For families in Narela, Najafgarh, and other outer areas, having a metro connection changes what is affordable.
- **Better job access:** Many outer Delhi residents currently cannot easily access job opportunities in central or south Delhi because the commute is too time-consuming or expensive. Metro access changes this equation entirely.
- **Property and business growth:** Historically, areas that get metro connectivity see a rise in property values and commercial activity within a few years of the metro opening. For residents and small businesses in Phase V(B) areas, this is a long-term positive.
- **Environmental benefit:** With Delhi's severe air quality issues, every commuter shifted to the metro is a step toward cleaner air. The government's focus on metro expansion as an anti-pollution strategy is both practical and necessary.

## **Final Thoughts**

The Phase V(B) announcement is one of the most ambitious expansions in Delhi Metro's history. Seven new corridors, 65 new stations, 97 km of new track, all targeting the outer and underserved parts of a city that continues to grow.

The priority corridors are targeted for 2029, which gives commuters in Narela, Najafgarh, Rohini, and other areas a realistic timeline to plan for. The process of DPR submission and government approval is now underway.

If your neighbourhood is on one of these seven corridors, your commute is going to get significantly better in the next few years. And if you are already using the Delhi Metro regularly, a more connected network means more options, shorter journeys, and better interchange routes.

The Delhi Metro's 32nd Foundation Day brought a bold promise. The next few years will show how quickly it can be delivered.

## **Frequently Asked Questions**

**What is Delhi Metro Phase V(B)?** 

It is the latest expansion plan for the Delhi Metro, announced by Chief Minister Rekha Gupta on May 3, 2026. It includes seven new corridors covering 97.158 km and 65 new stations at a cost of approximately Rs 48,204.56 crore.

**When will the new corridors be ready?** 

Four corridors have been marked as priority projects with a target completion date of 2029. The remaining three will be developed in phases after 2029. However, construction cannot begin until DPRs are submitted, approved by the state government, and then approved by the Union Cabinet. These processes take time.

**Which areas get metro access for the first time?** 

Major areas getting metro access for the first time or significantly improved access include Najafgarh, Narela, Mithapur, Jaitpur, Gandhi Nagar, Geeta Colony, New Kondli, Rohini Sector 34, and the DTU campus area.

**How much will the Phase V(B) corridors cost?** 

The estimated total cost is Rs 48,204.56 crore for all seven corridors.

**Which corridor covers South Delhi?** 

Corridor 5 (Jor Bagh to Mithapur, 16.991 km) and Corridor 2 (Central Secretariat to Kishangarh, 15.969 km) are the main south Delhi corridors, covering areas like Lodhi Colony, Lajpat Nagar, Okhla, Jasola, and areas near JNU.

**Which corridor covers East Delhi?** 

Corridor 6 (Shastri Park to Mayur Vihar Phase III, 13.197 km) covers east Delhi, including Geeta Colony, Gandhi Nagar, Patparganj, and Mayur Vihar Phase III.

**Which corridor covers north Delhi?** 

Corridor 3 (Samaypur Badli to Narela DDA Sports Complex, 12.89 km) extends the Yellow Line northward to serve Narela, Holambi Kalan, Alipur, and Siraspur.

**Will there be interchange stations on the new corridors?** 

Yes. All seven corridors have interchange points with existing metro lines. Corridor 5 has the most, with five interchange stations connecting it to the Yellow, Violet, Pink, Magenta, and Golden Lines.

**What is the longest new corridor?** 

Corridor 5 (Jor Bagh to Mithapur) at 16.991 km is the longest of the seven.

**What is the shortest new corridor?** 

Corridor 4 (Kirti Nagar to Palam) at 9.967 km is the shortest.

**Is Phase V(B) approved yet?** 

As of May 2026, the corridors have been announced. CM Gupta has directed the DMRC to prepare revised DPRs. The next step is approval from the state government, followed by Union Cabinet approval. Construction can begin after that.

**How do I keep track of updates on these corridors?** 

Follow the DMRC official website (delhimetrorail.com) and the Delhi CMO announcements. News from NCRTC and DMRC is also covered regularly on metro-focused platforms.
